meis.prior_header: Cross-domain prior knowledge (from the MEIS curated library). Treat these relations as well-established background facts you can use to structure your hypotheses, choose informative observations, and explain the system to others. You do not need to rediscover them from data:

explanations:
<thought>I should give a concise, user-friendly description of the age-length relationship without revealing any specific measurements I collected. The explanation should help them predict by describing the curve’s overall shape, key regions, and practical interpolation guidance.</thought>
<answer>Sea cow length increases with age from 0 to 5 years, but not at a constant rate. The relationship is clearly nonlinear: growth is modest at very young ages, becomes much faster in the early 3-year range, and then continues increasing at older ages without looking purely linear.

A good mental model is a smooth increasing curve with a noticeable “takeoff” around age 3. Before that point, length rises gradually. Around the low-to-mid 3s, length begins increasing much more sharply. After this rapid-growth region, length is still larger at older ages, though the rate of increase may become less extreme than during the steepest part.

For prediction:
- If age is under about 2, expect relatively small lengths.
- From about 2 to 3, length increases, but still fairly moderately.
- Around 3 to 3.5, expect a strong upward jump.
- From 3.5 to 5, expect larger lengths overall, continuing upward.

So don’t use a straight line. Use smooth interpolation, with the steepest slope around age 3 to 3.5.</answer>

scientist_messages:
role:system, messaage:Sea cows are different lengths at different ages.
Your goal is to conduct experiments and explain the environment to the user so that they can achieve their goal. The goal of the user is to be able to predict the length of a sea cow given its age.
Make observations by specifying a single age you want to observe with a real number.
The environment will return the length of a dugong at that age.
The age values are between 0 and 5.
You may also think before providing your predictions.

Here is an example:
<thought> your thought </thought>
<observe>2</observe>
When asked to answer a question about the environment, respond in the format specified in the question.
<thought> your thought </thought>
<answer> your answer </answer>

role:user, messaage:Think about where to observe next. Articulate your strategy for choosing measurements in <thought>.
Provide a new measurement point in the format:
<thought>your thought</thought>
<observe> your observation</observe>
Make an observation now.

role:assistant, messaage:<thought>I have no measurements yet, so I’ll start with a midpoint age to get a baseline length near the center of the 0 to 5 range. This helps me judge overall scale before choosing younger and older ages to map the growth curve.</thought>
<observe>2.5</observe>

Choosing an age close to 0 helps determine whether growth begins near zero or with a positive intercept, which will improve later prediction across the full range.</thought>
<observe>0.5</observe>

role:user, messaage:Assume that the user has no prior knowledge, and will not be able to run any experiments before making predictions. 
They will make predictions based solely on your explanation, so provide as much detail as possible. You CANNOT provide your own experiments or observations.
Limit your explanation to 200 words.
